data.title: Exhausted and forgiven Hong Kong striker aiming to avoid Singapore tears
data.description:
A jet-lagged and exhausted Michael Udebuluzor has vowed to be fit and firing for Hong Kong’s “do-or-die” Asian Cup qualifying clash with Singapore next Tuesday.
Striker Udebuluzor, who plays for Estonian top-flight side JK Tallinna Kalev, was replaced after 55 minutes of the 1-1 friendly draw with Cambodia on Thursday after he complained about breathing problems.
The 21-year-old was back in the squad following his exclusion from September and October get-togethers as punishment for calling Chinese supporters b******* after Hong Kong’s 1-0 East Asian finals defeat by China in July.
“I love coming back to Hong Kong. It feels good to wear the jersey again and I want to give everything,” Udebuluzor said. “I tried [against Cambodia], but I didn’t feel great, and I couldn’t breathe properly.
“I arrived on Sunday night and have barely slept. I’m still jet-lagged and exhausted. By Tuesday, my system will have started functioning properly.”
Udebuluzor acknowledged that the game with Singapore at a sold-out Kai Tak Stadium would be one of the biggest of his career.
The equation is simple: A win for Hong Kong would book their spot in the 2027 finals. Lose and Singapore go to Saudi Arabia instead. Only a draw would send the qualification race to the final round of matches next March.

“If we win, we qualify; if we lose, we cry, and I don’t want to cry,” Udebuluzor said.
“I want to play because I want to win for Hong Kong. There could be zero people watching, and I’d want to win.
“But, hopefully, we can use the 50,000 fans to our advantage. They will push us and make the other team nervous.”
Udebuluzor won the first of his 20 caps in September 2023, shortly after obtaining his passport.
He said he had not been surprised by the mushrooming interest in the city team in the intervening years, because “they told me the project before I came”.
Although only 7,480 showed up at unloved Hong Kong Stadium for the Cambodia fixture, that was more than the 5,289 who filed into the venue to watch Hong Kong beat the same opponents last October.
“If you win games, everything follows, and we’ve won a lot with the previous coach [Jorn Andersen], and now with Ashley [Westwood, current boss],” Udebuluzor said.
“Everything that’s happened is deserved. The city is with us and, hopefully, we will give back by winning on Tuesday. I think Hong Kong fans are the best in the world; I have never seen any other place like it.”

Westwood, meanwhile, absolved Udebuluzor of any blame for his enforced withdrawal, given he had played on Saturday in Estonia and travelled immediately afterwards.
Nonetheless, the Englishman said it was frustrating that “we don’t have lots of players who can play at the intensity we want for 90 minutes”.
“We’re narrowing down the players we can really rely on,” Westwood said.
“The last thing you want is to have to make substitutions for physical reasons. It’s always an issue at this level: you never know what players are doing every day at their clubs.
“You can’t gather enough information or data. That’s the job and you have to factor it into your decision making.”
With Oliver Gerbig suspended for Singapore, Shinichi Chan has emerged as the leading candidate to fill the hybrid defender-midfielder position.
“Shinichi is tall, fast, comfortable on the ball and a good defender,” Westwood said.
Westwood will choose between Tse Ka-Wing and 41-year-old Wang Zhenpeng in goal.
“It’s a flip of a coin; we have to trust one of them and, hopefully, it’s the right one,” Westwood said.

data.title: US-China tensions offer Asean ‘transformative’ critical minerals opportunity: economist
data.description:
Southeast Asian economies have a “transformative” opportunity to enter the world’s critical mineral supply chain amid China-US trade tensions, a senior Asian economist said.
Critical minerals – essential raw materials for the production of hi-tech gadgets, cars and aerospace equipment – will make the 11-member Association of Southeast Asian Nations’ (Asean) supply chain “more resilient and sustainable”, said Yasuto Watanabe, director of the Asean+3 Macroeconomic Research Office (AMRO).
The office was set up by the finance ministers of the Asean member states and the “plus three” – China, Japan and South Korea – following an agreement in 2009.
“Several member economies possess notable reserves and are actively exploring ways to integrate more deeply into the fast-growing clean-technology and advanced-manufacturing ecosystems,” Watanabe told the Post on Wednesday.
“Viewed through an ‘Asean plus three’ lens, the opportunity is transformative,” he said, with the Southeast Asian economies contributing resources and their “strategic location” to the critical minerals supply chain, and the other economies offering capital and technology.
In October, the United States signed memorandums of understanding with Asean members Malaysia and Thailand to secure critical minerals, namely the 17-member subset known as rare earth elements. These minerals are in particularly high demand for their utility in crafting permanent magnets and other technologically demanding components.
Malaysia already has processing capacity, setting it apart from other countries that just have reserves. Developing a mine from scratch to export capacity can take up to a decade, analysts have said.
The region must overcome … huge upfront costs in early-stage exploration
Deepali Bhargava, ING
The role of critical minerals in global supply chains received widespread attention this year when China – which accounts for about 70 per cent of global rare earth mining and 90 per cent of processing output, according to the Centre for Strategic and International Studies think tank – expanded the scope of its export controls on the materials.
The move alarmed Washington, hastening its search for alternative sources as it seeks to mitigate Beijing’s near-monopoly status, a source of leverage for China in the two countries’ prolonged trade war.
While Southeast Asian countries had a chance to produce some rare earth metals, given their extensive deposits, they would find it hard to “catch up” with China due to a lack of processing technology, said Liang Yan, a professor of economics at Willamette University in the US.
China might end up providing that technology, she said.
Beijing’s 12-year-old Belt and Road Initiative, which is focused on building trade-linked infrastructure abroad, was now helping Southeast Asian countries with “financing models” and technology, Watanabe said, adding that Beijing was pursuing infrastructure projects that “align more closely” with the needs of the region and its 700 million people.
This financing might come in the form of low-interest project loans, Liang said.
Deepali Bhargava, head of research and chief economist for Asia-Pacific with Dutch financial services firm ING, said access to funding would be essential for the region to make a difference in the minerals trade.
“To play a significant role in global [critical mineral] supply chains, the region must overcome … huge upfront costs in early-stage exploration and the need for funding across the entire value chain with a focus on sustaining autonomy and regional coordination.”

data.title: Anxiety to admiration: a day as a triathlon trainee with Hong Kong’s top coach
data.description:
While I maintain a regular functional fitness routine, let’s be honest – swimming, cycling and running have never been my strong suits.
So when the chance came up to train for one of the toughest sports – triathlon – with an experienced coach right here in Hong Kong, I felt nervously excited.
The opportunity presented itself last week when I met Andrew Wright, head coach of the Triathlon Association of Hong Kong, China, who has been training the city’s elite triathletes for the 15th National Games.
The squad representing Hong Kong – one of the host cities of the Games this year – includes Oscar Coggins, Robin Elg, Wong Tsz-to, Bailee Brown, Cade Wright and Hilda Choi Yan-yin. They will compete in individual races and the mixed relay at Central Harbourfront and Victoria Harbour on Saturday and Sunday.
I initially approached Andrew just asking for an amateur training plan – something I could quietly do in my own time.
But he took it a huge step further.
He offered to personally design a special session for me at the Hong Kong Sports Institute in Sha Tin, where the city’s best triathletes train, giving me a genuine, hands-on taste of elite sports conditioning.
I immediately accepted the challenge, though I knew there was no way I could complete a National Games-level triathlon.
Honestly, just thinking about the distances gives me anxiety.

The elite athletes are aiming for the podium in the main individual event, which comprises a gruelling 1,500-metre (4,921-feet) swim, a 40km (25 miles) bike ride and a 10km run.
My reality check: I’m not a strong swimmer, and finishing a 10km run alone is already a struggle. But getting a glimpse into the dedication and discipline required for such a high-stakes event was an opportunity I could not turn down.
Andrew explained that a full-time, national-level triathlete trained for about 30 hours a week – a regime that included roughly 25 to 30km of swimming, 10 to 14 hours of cycling, and 70 to 90km of running, plus a couple of gym sessions for muscle conditioning to prevent injury.
It’s a staggering workload that shows why he calls triathlon “probably one of the hardest sports out there”.
As daunted as I felt, Andrew reassured me: “I want to give you a taste of how elite athletes train. And I want you to fall in love with our sport so you keep coming back for more.”
He condensed the routine into a scaled-down version of the elite morning set: three rounds of a 100m swim followed by five minutes on the bike. The goal, he said, was to teach endurance, discipline and control.
After the first round, I was completely gassed. I had not been swimming for a while, and climbing out of the pool left me breathless.
He reminded me that consistency was the key to endurance – and that his job often involved motivating athletes to slow down.
“If you slow down and finish sessions day after day, week after week, then that’s where you really begin to see the improvements,” he said.
After the second round, I started feeling the burn in my quads from kicking too hard. I was struggling, but he kept me focused on discipline.
“Harder isn’t better. You don’t want to finish the session in a hole,” he added.
After the final round, Andrew performed an eye-opening blood lactate test to gauge my fitness level.
Lactate is produced during intense exercise when the body needs to generate energy quickly without oxygen – in other words, it is a key indicator of how hard the body is working.

While elite athletes typically record a lactate concentration of 3 to 4 mmol/L after a long, hard session – as they are trained to clear it efficiently – my result was a conclusive 16 mmol/L.
That high reading, a direct reflection of my lack of conditioning in these disciplines, was a humbling lesson.
My training day was not over yet. After a short recovery, I joined members of the Hong Kong team for running drills. Even after my scaled-down effort, I ended the day thoroughly exhausted but content.
I left the institute with immense respect for those dedicated to the sport, who train with precision and patience day after day.
The key takeaway? Triathlon is something almost anyone can take up.
As Andrew emphasised, the most important thing is consistency.
“You don’t need to do anything hard, anything specific, anything complicated. You just need to get out there and get some steps in basically or some lengths in the pool,” he said.

data.title: Andrew’s loss of ‘prince’ title echoes downfall of many a demoted royal in imperial China
data.description:
“The Andrew formerly known as Prince,” British tabloid The Sun announced on its front page on October 31. It was the cheekiest headline I have seen in a while.
Once a senior working royal with honorary military roles and public duties, Andrew, the younger brother of King Charles, was stripped of his patronages and barred from using “His Royal Highness” in 2022 after public outrage over his ties to child sex offender Jeffrey Epstein, and a civil settlement with Virginia Giuffre, who accused him of sexually abusing her when she was a teenager.
Last month, following the posthumous publication of Giuffre’s memoir six months after her suicide, Charles moved to strip Andrew of his princely title entirely. Henceforth, he will be known simply as “Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor”.
Though still technically in the line of succession, he has been reduced, in effect, to commoner status and exiled from public life.
In imperial China, princes of the blood did not often get demoted to commoner status, but when it happened, it usually followed palace intrigue or succession tussles. When a prince was perceived as a threat to their power, fathers, brothers, grandfathers and even mothers were ruthless in taking down their own flesh and blood.

Once stripped of rank, a prince’s fall was absolute. He lost his title and all the privileges that came with it. Without a state stipend, he could slip into poverty if he did not have a private income, like land or property.
However exalted his lineage, demotion from prince to commoner stripped him of his place in the imperial clan. His name was expunged from the clan register, severing him from the political influence and social prestige that came with belonging to the empire’s most exclusive circle.
His immediate family would not escape the fallout. Once a princely lineage fell, it usually stayed down. His children, grandchildren and their descendants could end up ordinary taxpayers, living among common folk for generations, boasting about their illustrious ancestor to anyone who would listen.
Humiliation and poverty were indignities to be suffered for sure, but the constant fear in which he lived his diminished life was the greater torment. His demotion to commoner status was often just the beginning of more terrible things to come.
The ex-prince would be constantly watched, whether he remained in the capital or was exiled to a distant corner of the realm, because his imperial bloodline would always be a potential political hazard. Even if he had no intention or ability to rebel, he could be a rallying point for insurgents to challenge the reigning emperor or whoever was in power.

His visitors and correspondence would be monitored, if they were not restricted altogether, to pre-empt any subversive plots. He might be confined within his quarters.
If it was decided that demotion, house arrest or even exile was insufficient to remove the political danger that the former prince engendered, there was always the ultimate option – death.
When the emperor, or the person acting in his name, “bestowed the gift of death” on an ex-prince, the latter was usually given the choice to take his own life, usually by taking poison or hanging himself. A refusal could bring swift execution by poison, strangulation or the sword.
Thankfully, such grim measures are no longer necessary, and Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor can live out his days in some comfort, albeit ignominiously, with financial support from Charles.
Yet in an age that prizes merit over bloodlines, at least in theory, the spectacle of an entire nation in a tizzy over a disgraced prince feels almost comic. If anything, Andrew’s fall reminds us that people like him are neither inherently superior nor preordained to demand our deference. They are royal only if we allow them to be.

data.title: Hong Kong triathletes ‘in great shape’, aim to turn National Games pressure into a positive
data.description:
Andrew Wright has conjured plans that give his triathletes the best chance of delivering results for Hong Kong, but the head coach acknowledged he was feeling the heat ahead of the marquee Central Harbourfront National Games races this weekend.
The city squad have three men and three women competing in Saturday’s individual events, before six are trimmed to four for the mixed relay on Sunday.
Wright said the men’s and relay races provided an equal chance of medals for Hong Kong, adding: “We know how the provinces are training and we’ve seen them in races, we’re very comparable to the top athletes there.”
Although he had no qualms about talking up his athletes’ podium prospects, Wright said he was “100 per cent” feeling the pressure.
“Acknowledging that and admitting it gives them confidence that they’re not in it on their own, I’m s******* myself, too, going into these things,” he said.
As anticipation heightened towards competition day, Wright was constantly reminding the athletes that pressure was a positive thing that “helps you perform rather than crushes you”.
“You have to get ahead of it, acknowledge it and reframe it,” he said.
“For some, this might be their last or only major Games, so I’m telling them to get some enjoyment out of it. If you can enjoy the whole process, you will perform better.”

All six of Wright’s squad were visibly in fabulous condition when they spoke to the media last week, following an intense five-week altitude camp in Yunnan.
Their chances of emerging from the individual competitions with something tangible to show for their efforts potentially rest on the shoulders of the gun men’s duo of Oscar Coggins and Robin Elg, who were fourth and fifth, respectively, in August’s Asia Triathlon Championships.
The leading women’s performer, Bailee Brown, has recovered her fitness and natural ebullience after a nine-month injury ordeal.
“Every person is as important as the next one, but having your strongest team on race day, which I believe we have, is super important,” Wright said. “I’m so happy everyone is fit and ready to go.”
Wright accepted that it was very important the triathletes performed on home ground, because “it would keep the spotlight on the sport, keep people interested and drive participation, which is really lacking in Hong Kong”.
He also believed that greater scrutiny fed into increased accountability and better performances.
The plan Wright’s men have been asked to execute on Saturday involved a blistering start that would cash in on Coggins and Elg’s swimming and biking prowess, and minimised the influence of their weaker running suit. Wong Tsz-to’s strength lay in his foot speed, so his task was to avoid becoming detached before the closing run.

Brown, Cade Wright and Hilda Choi Yan-yin have worked furiously on their swims to ensure they were part of the race as it began to play to their biking and running specialities.
“All we’re asking is that they do their best to deliver their race strategies,” Wright said.
“Something’s clicked over the past six months; they’ve trained at their highest level and conducted themselves professionally. They’re all in great shape.”
When the talking stops and the action starts, on a technical course that Wright said gave his athletes home advantage, the coach is powerless to do anything other than “sit tight”.
“Once the athletes go, a lot of coaches pretend they’re doing something, but they’re not,” Wright said.
“If you get an opportunity to give a bit of motivation, you try, but most of the time they can’t hear you.”

data.title: Climate collapse: Southeast Asia’s new normal? ‘Everybody will be giving up’
data.description:
For four long months and counting, the residents of Bang Ban, a low-lying district in Thailand’s ancient city of Ayutthaya, have lived their lives under water
Elderly residents must be ferried to safety by raft, schools stand closed and under the muddy surface, ruined rice fields rot.
Even Ayutthaya’s 700-year-old stupas have been submerged, their crumbling towers jutting through a murky brown deluge that shows no sign of retreat.

Annual floods are not unusual here. Each monsoon season, the Chao Phraya River – Thailand’s largest – routinely bursts its banks and Bang Ban often bears the burden of diverted water when upstream dams cannot cope.
But this year is different. The scale is catastrophic, says local politician and former rescuer Songphol Suksomboon.
“There’s so much water they don’t know what to do with it,” he told This Week in Asia. And more is on the way.
Thailand’s water authority on Monday increased the outflow from upstream dams to 2,900 cubic metres (102,000 cubic feet) per second, all but guaranteeing that Bang Ban’s ordeal will continue.

When the waters finally do recede, a new set of hardships will begin. The first, says Songphol, is the cost and toil of clean-up: a familiar burden in one of Southeast Asia’s most flood-prone regions. But then comes the changing season’s next cruel turn.
“Once the rains stop, the summer comes in and it’s time for drought, which kills the crops,” he said.
Droughts in Thailand are as punishing as the floods, decimating harvests, plunging farmers into debt and threatening food security.
It’s a cycle playing out across Southeast Asia – a region whose climate crisis is, in effect, not just one of too much water or too little, but of relentless extremes.

‘A fighting chance’?
Scientists warn that as the planet warms, monsoons are intensifying and typhoons are growing more frequent and more violent.
The Philippines and Vietnam have endured a near-unbroken succession of storms this year, killing hundreds and devastating homes and farmlands, leaving communities little if any time to recover.
Thailand, Indonesia, Myanmar and Laos have also suffered through higher-than-usual rainfall, though their major cities have so far escaped ruinous floods.
With a population of barely 10,000, Bang Ban rarely features in the global climate debate. Yet its story cuts to the heart of it.
It’s not just about targets any more; it’s about trust, timelines and tangible transitions
Dr Renard Siew, climate change researcher
As scientists warn that the world may already be near the point of irreversible climate breakdown, the absence of key global powers from this month’s Cop30 talks in Brazil hangs heavy.
US President Donald Trump has dismissed the crisis as a “green scam”, reneging on his country’s climate commitments, and neither the United States, China nor India – the world’s top three emitters – have sent senior representatives to host city Belem.
Meanwhile, the once-accepted goal of limiting global warming to 1.5 degrees Celsius slips ever further out of reach.
Scientists are sounding the alarm. “What’s decided in Belem will define whether we still have a fighting chance,” said Dr Renard Siew, a sustainability and climate expert at Malaysia’s University Tunku Abdul Rahman, speaking from the summit.
“It’s not just about targets any more; it’s about trust, timelines and tangible transitions, because if Cop30 doesn’t accelerate credible action, 1.5 degrees moves from being a goal to a eulogy.”

Mounting costs
The consequences of inaction are profound. Each disaster leaves governments across Southeast Asia scrambling to rebuild, draining budgets and pushing cities to the edge of insurability.
Between 1970 and 2021, weather-related events cost the region an estimated US$1.4 trillion.
The Asian Development Bank warned in March that, without urgent mitigation, climate change could erase up to 17 per cent of Southeast Asia’s gross domestic product by 2070 – and 41 per cent by the end of the century.
In Vietnam, where a 3,200km (1,990-mile) coastline stands exposed to typhoons born over a warming Pacific, the effects are already visible. In Hoi An, the Unesco-listed town famed for its yellow shopfronts and lantern-lit streets, last month’s floods left cafes and boutiques caked in a layer of treacle-thick mud.

“It was a week of cleaning and cleaning again,” said coffee shop owner Doan Thanh Tuan, whose business suffered about US$3,500 in losses.
“The weather is becoming more and more unpredictable,” she told This Week in Asia. “If the damage is too much to bear, I might have to close down, as some shops already have.”
As Southeast Asia’s seas warm, the cost to its irreplaceable natural heritage is also climbing.
In Thailand’s Andaman Sea, the seagrass meadows that nourish endangered dugongs are dying off. Hawksbill turtles and Irrawaddy dolphins are also disappearing.

Malaysia’s coral reefs – the jewels of dive sites from Sipadan to Mabul – are bleaching, and the ancient mangrove forests of Indonesia’s North Kalimantan and the Philippines’ Palawan, for centuries nature’s flood barriers against storm surges, are dying or being cleared for development.
Yet water has become the unexpected centrepiece of this year’s Cop30 talks, even if the absence of the world’s top emitters raises uncomfortable questions about the effectiveness of what is discussed.
On Tuesday, the UN climate body UNFCCC announced a Joint Statement on Water and Climate Action, pledging to prioritise water management, drought and flood mitigation, and access to clean water, laying the groundwork for a global water summit in the United Arab Emirates next year.

Living the collapse
While the delegates debate in Brazil, the Philippines is enduring the consequences of the climate collapse in real time.
Already this year, 21 tropical cyclones have battered the archipelago, two of them classed as super typhoons, with winds exceeding 185km/h (115mph) – roughly equivalent to a Category 4 or 5 hurricane.
Typhoon Fung-wong displaced 1.4 million people as it made landfall last Sunday just days after Typhoon Kalmaegi had torn through the country. Hundreds have been killed, homes and farmland destroyed, and entire communities forced to start again.
“Super Typhoon Fung-wong is one of the most destructive typhoons to hit Pangasinan in recent years,” said Francisco Dangla, a Filipino climate activist who works closely with fisherfolk and marginalised communities along the coast of Pangasinan province.
Surging floodwaters, some more than 1.2 metres high, ravaged homes and fishing boats alike.


“Worse still, many small fishing boats were also damaged by the storm surge,” he added, leaving coastal families with no insurance dependent on aid just to return to sea and their livelihoods.
For an already impoverished province, the blow was devastating. Official figures show the poverty rate in Pangasinan climbed from 9.3 per cent in 2018 to 13.9 per cent in 2021, with nearly half a million people surviving on less than US$1.50 a day.
As the rhetoric at Cop30 grows ever more urgent amid the absence of the world’s biggest emitters and a climate-denying president in the White House, many fear the window for meaningful action is closing fast.
Patricia Espinosa, a former executive secretary of the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change, told this Week in Asia that Cop30 was “very urgent, very critical”.
If momentum was lost “everybody will be giving up on actions regarding climate change”, the ex-UN climate chief said.
For Bang Ban and countless places like it, that warning is already a lived reality.

data.title: Be mindful of trail runners passing Hong Kong’s Sunset Peak, hikers told
data.description:
Hong Kong authorities have warned hikers to be mindful of people taking part in a high-profile trail running race on Lantau Island over the weekend, as the route passes through a popular sightseeing spot.
The Agricultural, Fisheries and Conservation Department (AFCD) on Friday warned hikers that large crowds were expected on Sunset Peak and other routes during the biggest trail race on Lantau.
“Participants will be passing through Sunset Peak, a popular spot for viewing silver grass, which is along section two of the Lantau Trail,” the department said in a post on its Hong Kong Country Parks social media account.
“Keep left, walk in a single file while hiking and be mindful of oncoming runners.”
It also reminded hikers to be considerate, keep the noise down and stay on the path to avoid trampling on the silver grass.
Participants in three of the four TransLantau races organised by UTMB, which take place over the weekend, will pass by section two of the trail.
The department said it expected the peak periods to be between 10am and 4pm on Saturday, and from 11am to 4pm on Sunday.
The mountain event will see runners taking on four challenging races of distances spanning 25km to 120km (15.5 miles to 74.6 miles) between Friday and Sunday.
But crowds started flocking to Hong Kong’s third-highest peak last weekend as temperatures dipped to a comfortable autumn chill.
Dozens of posts on Chinese social media platform RedNote gave tips on how to reach Sunset Peak to catch the mountain top in its full glory during the silver-grass season.
Fans of Hong Kong singer Eason Chan are also known to flock to the mountain to recreate the cover shot of his 2010 album, Taste the Atmosphere.
But images of rubbish littering the countryside and hikers climbing up and jumping on the roofs of the Lantau Mountain Camp cabins posted last weekend have drawn the ire of internet users.
Other pictures circulating online show a long line of hikers stretching across several slopes along the route, their headlamps lit as night falls, and a long queue at Pak Kung Au bus stop for public transport back to Tung Chung.

The department said that its staff had been enhancing patrol and educational efforts since early November by promoting hiking etiquette, particularly messages about using the designated trail to avoid trampling the silver grass and keeping the countryside clean.
It added that it had also increased litter collection services along the trail and stepped up cleaning of portable toilets along the Pak Kung Au and Nam Shan trailheads.
“Recently, the AFCD have not received any complaints from the owners of the Lantau Mountain Camp regarding disturbances caused by visitors,” it said.
The Transport Department said it was closely monitoring the demand for transport to Sunset Peak and had asked the New Lantao Bus (NLB) Company to boost its services.
“NLB has already improved the frequency of bus routes to Tung Chung, such as route 3M, 11 and 23, during the evening peak period, when visitors started to leave Pak Kung Au,” the department said.
“The combined frequency for these routes has been strengthened from about five minutes per bus to about three minutes per bus over the past weekend, and NLB will continue to implement such arrangements over the coming weekends.”
It added that the operator also arranged for 10 spare buses to ferry passengers from Pak Kung Au during the peak period to cater for the sudden upsurge in demand.

data.title: US funds tread carefully on China as Oaktree and Appaloosa recalibrate positions
data.description:
Major US fund managers stayed cautious on China in the third quarter, with Oaktree Capital Management rebalancing its exposure to both equities and convertible bonds tied to Chinese firms, while Appaloosa – founded by billionaire investor David Tepper – made selective adjustments.
Oaktree Capital, founded by notable investor Howard Marks, sold all of its 1.5 million shares valued at US$26.8 million in KE Holdings, a Chinese online property platform, in the third quarter, according to its latest 13F filing with the US Securities and Exchange Commission.
It also pared back its exposure to several convertible bonds issued by Chinese firms. The fund exited its US$6.9 million position in convertible bonds issued by e-commerce giant Alibaba Group Holding, as well as a US$7.3 million stake in bonds from hotel chain operator H World Group. Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.
By contrast, Oaktree Capital boosted its positions in online recruitment service provider Kanzhun by 72,300 shares, or 5 per cent, to US$38.4 million from US$28 million at the end of June. It also increased its holdings in convertible bonds issued by electric-vehicle maker Li Auto by 121 per cent, e-commerce firm JD.com by 93 per cent, and online travel-booking agency Trip.com by 6 per cent, in terms of principal amount.

Appaloosa took a similarly nuanced approach. The hedge fund increased its stake in Baidu by 420,000 shares to just over 1 million shares, boosting the position’s market value to US$137.7 million from US$53.6 million a quarter earlier.
It exited all its positions in KE Holdings, selling its stake worth US$26.6 million, and trimmed its position in Alibaba by around 617,000 shares, or 9 per cent, while still maintaining a substantial US$1.15 billion stake in the e-commerce giant at the end of September. It also reduced its holdings in Pinduoduo by 10 per cent and JD.com by 11 per cent.
Appaloosa’s filing reflects Tepper’s recalibration of his China exposure, suggesting a moderated commitment to Chinese assets. Tepper, known for his aggressive and often contrarian strategies, had famously declared in 2024 that he would buy “everything” China-related amid a bull run starting in late September when Beijing unveiled a slew of measures to boost the economy.
The moves suggest a prudent approach by top funds amid trade tensions between the US and China since April, despite a bull run in Chinese equities this year.
Bridgewater Associates, the world’s largest hedge fund, stayed away from China in the third quarter, maintaining zero direct exposure to US-listed Chinese companies since the second quarter.
The firm had liquidated its entire US$1.4 billion China stock portfolio in the second quarter, including stakes in Alibaba, JD.com, Baidu, Nio and Yum China. The sell-off also included TAL Education Group, H World Group, KE Holdings and Autohome, wiping out Bridgewater’s direct exposure to US-traded Chinese equities for the first time in years.

data.title: How vertical dramas, born in China, are disrupting Hollywood, one minute at a time
data.description:
In a faux castle on a hill in the US city of Los Angeles, a small film crew – with their cameras flipped sideways – squeeze into a bedroom and shoot a raunchy affair scene under the gaze of their Chinese producer.
This is the brave new world of vertical dramas – also called micro dramas – a multibillion-dollar industry that has taken Hollywood by storm in just two years, churning out algorithm-driven movies that are watched in addictive 60-second instalments on smartphones.
With kitschy plots about werewolves and billionaires, budgets of just a few hundred thousand dollars and breakneck filming schedules, this format from China is radically changing – and some say, saving – a struggling industry.
Vertical dramas are like a “soap opera on cocaine”, says producer Vincent Wang.
“In 30 days, we can get a show together. Hollywood takes two years. We have already made 500 shows by the time they make their first. Who is the future?”

Vertical dramas emerged in China in the 2010s, initially shot by amateurs and shared on TikTok. Big companies swept in, recognising the potential for rapid financial returns when hastily and cheaply produced micro dramas go viral.
“I honestly think it’s the future … right here, right now,” says Zachary Shadrin, a television and film actor, making his vertical drama debut in Love Through All Seasons.
“It’s a good time to jump on this train.”
Like many of his Los Angeles-based contemporaries, Shadrin was initially wary of vertical dramas.
Aside from intense filming schedules – vertical films can wrap in as few as five days – the genre is infamous for its fixation with abusive relationships and violent male protagonists.
“I personally think it’s toxic,” says Shadrin. But he agreed to star in FlareFlow’s Love Through All Seasons because of the age-gap romcom’s “sweet” premise.

“It wasn’t something I saw usually in terms of verticals,” he says, expressing hope that the quality of scripts is rising.
Indeed, multiple actors who recently moved to vertical films have said they were pleasantly surprised by the professionalism on sets.
“We all chuckle at some of the lines that are absolutely ridiculous,” said actor Nicholas McDonald.
“But everyone’s a good sport and treats it very professionally. Because there’s money behind it.”
Producers of vertical dramas say they are not competing with HBO or Netflix for eyeballs, but rather with TikTok, YouTube and Instagram.
The business model is radical but simple.

Vertical-drama platforms offer the first six to 12 mini-episodes for free before requiring customers to pay to keep going.
So each instalment, despite being barely a minute long, has to contain its own emotional twist to prevent its viewer from doom-scrolling elsewhere. Plots rely heavily on genre tropes such as vampires and werewolves.
With the snappy format and storylines, “the audience can be hooked right away without using too much of their brain,” says Love Through All Seasons director Li Weiyang.
“Everyone’s exhausted from their life already.”

Companies scrutinise viewing data to see which plot devices have worked best, enabling them to churn out dozens more shows along those lines within months.
Production frequently begins before screenplays have been completed. Many scripts are translated from existing Chinese hits. And artificial intelligence is sometimes used to generate ideas, says FlareFlow screenwriter Qu Zhiyuan.
Another cost advantage? The vertical format suited to smartphone screens means less background is visible on either side of the actors, which means cheaper set dressing and fewer crew.
After years in which Hollywood sets have closed due to Covid-19 pandemic lockdowns, strikes and tax incentives that lure productions elsewhere, the allure of vertical shorts is too strong in the US.
“I can ditch my side gigs, my support job, so that I can go and act … that’s cool,” says McDonald, who estimates 80 per cent of his auditions are currently for vertical dramas.
Vertical dramas are “creeping out from the shadows”, he says.

data.title: China’s commitment to globalisation evident at import expo
data.description:
China’s drive to raise domestic consumption’s contribution to economic growth got a big boost from the annual China International Import Expo (CIIE) in Shanghai. Mainland companies signed a record US$83.5 billion in deals with exporters from a wide range of countries and regions, up 4.4 per cent on the previous year.
The contracts were preliminary, and some deals might not be finalised while many were pre-signed. Even so, the six-day annual expo, in its eighth year since being launched in 2018, was the catalyst, along with the recent truce agreed on by President Xi Jinping and US President Donald Trump in the trade war between China and the United States. The result is evidence enough of the importance of mainland consumers as growth drivers for global business and the world economy.
The surge in deals reflected pent-up demand and supply during the trade war downturn. It also showcased Beijing’s pledge to further open up.
Amid a slowing world economy and geopolitical tensions, the stability and security of supply chains are crucial to growth. In his keynote speech at the expo opening, Premier Li Qiang stressed that China was willing to work with all parties to ensure the stability of global industrial and supply chains.
The expo followed the fourth plenary session of the 20th Communist Party Central Committee, which emphasised deepening “reform and opening up” as essential pathways towards China’s modernisation and sustainable growth.
The expo reflected China’s commitment to economic opening and globalisation. By providing a platform for countries and businesses worldwide to showcase their products and services, the CIIE has become a pivotal event for promoting international trade and investment.
In terms of broader economic reform, the expo aligns with the goal of deepening the country’s integration into the global economy. By embracing a more open trade environment, China also strengthens its role as a facilitator of global dialogue and cooperation.
The world’s largest import fair has become a vital platform for Hong Kong firms to bypass traditional market entry barriers, forge new cross-border and overseas investor connections and secure business deals in mainland China, according to successful exhibitors from the city. They said they had not only secured new contacts with mainland manufacturers but even received investment inquiries from different cities. A record 380 Hong Kong companies from various industries took part in the show this year, a 30 per cent increase from 2024.
